Le contenu du panel settings ne me va pas, j'aimerais qu'on le revoit ensemble:

# General

- Startup at login => a mettre en tout premier
- Déplacer le log level à la fin de ce tab (supprimer le tab "Logging")
- Polling refresh interval => à déplacer dans un nouvel onglet "Assistants"

# Menubar hint (nouveau)

Cette partie est destinée à configurer les "segments" qu'on souhaite afficher dans la menubar
Un segment target une métrique donnée, c'est par exemple "[pastille metrique][lettre metrique][pourcentage metrique][temps restant d'ici au prochain reset]"

Quand on affiche "[pastille verte]S 2% 1h47m | [pastille jaune]W 48% 3d 11h47m" on affiche 2 segments

On a une liste de segments affichés qui correspond à ce que l'utilisateur a actuellement de paramétré. Un bouton permet à l'utilisateur de créer un nouveau segment en draft.
Chaque segment est en fait une section expandable dont le titre donne le rendu actuel dudit segment, ex: "[pastille verte]S 2% 1h47m"
Quand on expand, on accède à la configuration de chaque segment:
- Vendor: dropdown avec tous les vendors unique du usages.json (uniquement "claude" pour le moment, mais ça évoluera dans le futur)
- Account: dropdown avec tous les accounts pour le vendor sélectionné du usages.json
- Metric: dropdown avec toutes les métriques pour l'account+vendor sélectionné du usages.json
- "Afficher la pastille": toggle on/off
- "Afficher une lettre correspondant à la métrique": toggle on/off
- "Afficher le pourcentage d'utilisation": toggle on/off
- "Afficher le temps restant d'ici au prochain reset" toggle on/off



# Assistants (nouveau)

Faire une Section nommée "Claude"

Y mettre un seul champ pour le API Polling interval
Faire un seul champ (plutot que 2 actuellement), de type slider, avec 5 valeurs possibles: 1 minute, 2 minutes, 3 minutes, 5 minutes, 10 minutes.
Par défaut, au tout premier lancement de l'app, c'est 3 minutes qui est configuré
S'assurer que ce paramétrage est bien utilisé par le Claude Connector pour ses refresh API

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- archon: Use when: User wants to run Archon workflows, CREATE workflows or commands,
set up Archon, or manage Archon configuration.
Triggers (run): "use archon to", "run archon", "archon workflow", "use archon for",
          "have archon", "let archon", "ask archon to".
Triggers (create): "create a workflow", "write a workflow", "make a command",
          "author a workflow", "new workflow", "new command", "archon workflow yaml".
Triggers (setup): "set up archon", "install archon", "how to use archon",
          "configure archon", "archon setup", "get started with archon".
Triggers (config): "change my archon config", "modify archon config", "archon config",
          "change archon settings", "update my config", "help me change my config",
          "edit archon config", "archon configuration".
Triggers (init): "initialize archon", "set up .archon", "archon init", "add archon to repo".
Capability: Runs AI workflows in isolated git worktrees for parallel development.
Also: Creates and manages workflow YAML files, command files, and configuration.
NOT for: Direct Claude Code work - only for delegating to Archon CLI.
- dbg: Debug applications using the dbg CLI debugger. Supports Node.js (V8/CDP), Bun (WebKit/JSC), and native code via LLDB (DAP). Use when: (1) investigating runtime bugs by stepping through code, (2) inspecting variable values at specific execution points, (3) setting breakpoints and conditional breakpoints, (4) evaluating expressions in a paused context, (5) hot-patching code without restarting (JS/TS), (6) debugging test failures by attaching to a running process, (7) debugging C/C++/Rust/Swift with LLDB, (8) any task where understanding runtime behavior requires a debugger. Triggers: "debug this", "set a breakpoint", "step through", "inspect variables", "why is this value wrong", "trace execution", "attach debugger", "runtime error", "segfault", "core dump".
- tech-debt: Create a new technical debt entry in the tech-debt/ directory. Use when identifying technical debt to formalize, track, or document for future resolution.
- improve-ai-harness: Analyze review feedbacks or development friction points and suggest improvements to the AI harness (rules, CLAUDE.md, docs, tooling). Use when asked to improve AI rules, after a code review with issues, or to strengthen the AI configuration.
- roadmap-feature: Create a new epic entry in the roadmap/ directory. Use when the user wants to plan a new feature, add an item to the roadmap, or formalize an epic for future implementation.
- session-save: Save the current session state — decisions, modified files, current status, and next steps — to a handoff file for later resume.
- learn-from-mistakes: Now that you learnt some new stuff, write detailed specs and reduce ambiguity before handing work...

- pr-context: Load the full context of a GitHub Pull Request so the user can ask questions about it.
- deep-init:run: Generate comprehensive codebase documentation with nested CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md and architecture docs

1	import AppKit
2	import AIUsagesTrackersLib
3	
4	/// Rasterises the full menu bar label into a non-template `NSImage`.
5	/// MenuBarExtra's label was unusable: template tinting stripped the per-tier
6	/// dot colors, and mixed HStacks silently truncated segments past the first.
7	/// With a custom `NSStatusItem`, we control `isTemplate = false` so hues
8	/// survive and we pick the text color from the status button's effective
9	/// appearance (which tracks the menu bar, not the app).
10	@MainActor
11	enum MenuBarLabelRenderer {
12	    private static let font: NSFont = .menuBarFont(ofSize: 0)
13	    private static let separator = " | "
14	    private static let dot = "● "
15	    private static let horizontalPadding: CGFloat = 4
16	    private static let height: CGFloat = 18
17	
18	    static func render(
19	        segments: [MenuBarSegment],
20	        fallbackText: String,
21	        isDarkMenuBar: Bool
22	    ) -> NSImage {
23	        let textColor: NSColor = isDarkMenuBar ? .white : .black
24	        let attributed = attributedString(
25	            segments: segments,
26	            fallbackText: fallbackText,
27	            textColor: textColor
28	        )
29	        let textSize = attributed.size()
30	        let width = ceil(textSize.width) + horizontalPadding * 2
31	        let size = NSSize(width: max(width, 1), height: height)
32	        let image = NSImage(size: size)
33	        image.lockFocus()
34	        let textY = (height - ceil(textSize.height)) / 2
35	        attributed.draw(at: NSPoint(x: horizontalPadding, y: textY))
36	        image.unlockFocus()
37	        image.isTemplate = false
38	        return image
39	    }
40	
41	    private static func attributedString(
42	        segments: [MenuBarSegment],
43	        fallbackText: String,
44	        textColor: NSColor
45	    ) -> NSAttributedString {
46	        guard !segments.isEmpty else {
47	            return NSAttributedString(
48	                string: fallbackText,
49	                attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
50	            )
51	        }
52	        let result = NSMutableAttributedString()
53	        for (index, segment) in segments.enumerated() {
54	            if index > 0 {
55	                result.append(NSAttributedString(
56	                    string: separator,
57	                    attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
58	                ))
59	            }
60	            let dotColor = segment.tier?.nsColor ?? textColor
61	            result.append(NSAttributedString(
62	                string: dot,
63	                attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: dotColor]
64	            ))
65	            result.append(NSAttributedString(
66	                string: segment.text,
67	                attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
68	            ))
69	        }
70	        return result
71	    }
72	}

1	import AppKit
2	import SwiftUI
3	import AIUsagesTrackersLib
4	
5	@MainActor
6	final class AppDelegate: NSObject, NSApplicationDelegate {
7	    private var poller: UsagePoller?
8	    private var accountMonitor: ClaudeActiveAccountMonitor?
9	    private var pidGuard: AppPidGuard?
10	    private var usageStore: UsageStore?
11	    private var refreshState: RefreshState?
12	    private var statusItem: NSStatusItem?
13	    private var popover: NSPopover?
14	    private var appearanceObserver: NSKeyValueObservation?
15	    private var settingsWindowObserver: NSObjectProtocol?
16	
17	    /// Shared preferences store — exposed as static so the SwiftUI Settings scene
18	    /// (constructed before applicationDidFinishLaunching) can access it.
19	    static let sharedPreferences: UserDefaultsAppPreferences = UserDefaultsAppPreferences()
20	
21	    func applicationDidFinishLaunching(_ notification: Notification) {
22	        NSApplication.shared.setActivationPolicy(.accessory)
23	
24	        let home = FileManager.default.homeDirectoryForCurrentUser.path
25	        let guard_ = AppPidGuard(cacheDir: "\(home)/.cache/ai-usages-tracker")
26	        do {
27	            try guard_.acquire()
28	        } catch AppPidGuardError.alreadyRunning(let pid, _) {
29	            let alert = NSAlert()
30	            alert.messageText = "AI Usages Tracker is already running"
31	            alert.informativeText = "Another instance is active (PID \(pid)). Use the menu bar icon to quit it before launching a new one."
32	            alert.runModal()
33	            NSApplication.shared.terminate(nil)
34	            return
35	        } catch {
36	            let alert = NSAlert()
37	            alert.messageText = "Failed to start AI Usages Tracker"
38	            alert.informativeText = error.localizedDescription
39	            alert.runModal()
40	            NSApplication.shared.terminate(nil)
41	            return
42	        }
43	        pidGuard = guard_
44	
45	        Loggers.setPreferences(Self.sharedPreferences)
46	
47	        // Reconcile launch-at-login preference with system state — the user may
48	        // have disabled the entry in System Settings > Login Items directly.
49	        let launchService = LaunchAtLoginService.shared
50	        if Self.sharedPreferences.launchAtLogin != launchService.isEnabled {
51	            Self.sharedPreferences.launchAtLogin = launchService.isEnabled
52	        }
53	
54	        let fileManager = UsagesFileManager.shared
55	        let refreshState = RefreshState()
56	        let poller = UsagePoller(
57	            connectors: [ClaudeCodeConnector()],
58	            fileManager: fileManager,
59	            refreshState: refreshState,
60	            preferences: Self.sharedPreferences
61	        )
62	        let accountMonitor = ClaudeActiveAccountMonitor(
63	            fileManager: fileManager,
64	            onActiveAccountChanged: { [weak poller] _ in
65	                await poller?.pollOnce(force: true)
66	            }
67	        )
68	        self.poller = poller
69	        self.accountMonitor = accountMonitor
70	        self.refreshState = refreshState
71	
72	        let usagesPath = fileManager.filePath
73	        let fileWatcher = UsagesFileWatcher(path: usagesPath)
74	        let store = UsageStore(fileWatcher: fileWatcher)
75	        self.usageStore = store
76	
77	        setupStatusItem(store: store, refreshState: refreshState)
78	        trackStoreChanges(store: store)
79	
80	        Task {
81	            store.start()
82	            await poller.start()
83	            await accountMonitor.start()
84	        }
85	    }
86	
87	    func applicationWillTerminate(_ notification: Notification) {
88	        quit()
89	    }
90	
91	    // MARK: - Status item
92	
93	    private func setupStatusItem(store: UsageStore, refreshState: RefreshState) {
94	        let item = NSStatusBar.system.statusItem(withLength: NSStatusItem.variableLength)
95	        statusItem = item
96	
97	        let popover = NSPopover()
98	        popover.behavior = .transient
99	        let hosting = NSHostingController(
100	            rootView: UsageDetailsView(
101	                store: store,
102	                refreshState: refreshState,
103	                onRefresh: { [weak self] in
104	                    await self?.poller?.pollOnce(now: Date(), force: true)
105	                },
106	                onOpenSettings: { [weak self] in
107	                    self?.openSettings()
108	                },
109	                onQuit: { [weak self] in
110	                    self?.quit()
111	                }
112	            )
113	        )
114	        // Let SwiftUI's intrinsic size drive the popover so it grows with content
115	        // up to the screen-ratio cap enforced inside UsageDetailsView.
116	        hosting.sizingOptions = [.preferredContentSize]
117	        popover.contentViewController = hosting
118	        self.popover = popover
119	
120	        if let button = item.button {
121	            button.target = self
122	            button.action = #selector(togglePopover(_:))
123	            // The menu bar appearance can change (wallpaper-driven tinting) without
124	            // the app's effectiveAppearance changing, so we observe the button itself.
125	            appearanceObserver = button.observe(\.effectiveAppearance, options: [.new]) { [weak self] _, _ in
126	                Task { @MainActor in self?.refreshStatusItemImage() }
127	            }
128	        }
129	
130	        refreshStatusItemImage()
131	    }
132	
133	    private func refreshStatusItemImage() {
134	        guard let store = usageStore, let button = statusItem?.button else { return }
135	        let isDark = button.effectiveAppearance.bestMatch(from: [.darkAqua, .aqua]) == .darkAqua
136	        let image = MenuBarLabelRenderer.render(
137	            segments: store.menuBarSegments,
138	            fallbackText: store.menuBarText,
139	            isDarkMenuBar: isDark
140	        )
141	        button.image = image
142	    }
143	
144	    @objc private func togglePopover(_ sender: Any?) {
145	        guard let popover, let button = statusItem?.button else { return }
146	        if popover.isShown {
147	            popover.performClose(sender)
148	        } else {
149	            popover.show(relativeTo: button.bounds, of: button, preferredEdge: .minY)
150	            popover.contentViewController?.view.window?.makeKey()
151	        }
152	    }
153	
154	    // MARK: - Observation
155	
156	    /// Re-arms `withObservationTracking` after every change so the status item
157	    /// keeps mirroring the store. Tracking fires exactly once per registration.
158	    private func trackStoreChanges(store: UsageStore) {
159	        withObservationTracking {
160	            _ = store.menuBarSegments
161	            _ = store.menuBarText
162	        } onChange: { [weak self] in
163	            Task { @MainActor in
164	                guard let self, let store = self.usageStore else { return }
165	                self.refreshStatusItemImage()
166	                self.trackStoreChanges(store: store)
167	            }
168	        }
169	    }
170	
171	    // MARK: - Settings
172	
173	    private func openSettings() {
174	        popover?.performClose(nil)
175	        // .accessory apps have no dock presence; showSettingsWindow: silently fails
176	        // unless we temporarily switch to .regular so AppKit can make the window key.
177	        NSApp.setActivationPolicy(.regular)
178	        NSApp.activate(ignoringOtherApps: true)
179	
180	        // SwiftUI creates the Settings window lazily on first showSettingsWindow: —
181	        // if it already exists, reuse it directly to avoid responder-chain races
182	        // that occur during the popover-dismissal + activation-policy transition.
183	        if let settingsWindow = Self.findSettingsWindow() {
184	            settingsWindow.makeKeyAndOrderFront(nil)
185	        } else {
186	            // First-time path: AppKit needs a runloop tick after the .accessory →
187	            // .regular transition to install the standard App menu (which is where
188	            // SwiftUI wires the Settings… action). sendAction with a nil target
189	            // relies on that wiring, so we trigger the menu item directly once
190	            // AppKit has had a chance to build it.
191	            DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + 0.15) {
192	                Self.triggerSettingsMenuItem()
193	                if let settingsWindow = Self.findSettingsWindow() {
194	                    settingsWindow.makeKeyAndOrderFront(nil)
195	                }
196	            }
197	        }
198	
199	        // Restore .accessory policy once the settings window is closed.
200	        if settingsWindowObserver == nil {
201	            settingsWindowObserver = NotificationCenter.default.addObserver(
202	                forName: NSWindow.willCloseNotification,
203	                object: nil,
204	                queue: .main
205	            ) { [weak self] _ in
206	                guard let self else { return }
207	                Task { @MainActor in
208	                    let hasVisibleWindows = NSApp.windows.contains { $0.isVisible }
209	                    if !hasVisibleWindows {
210	                        NSApp.setActivationPolicy(.accessory)
211	                        if let token = self.settingsWindowObserver {
212	                            NotificationCenter.default.removeObserver(token)
213	                            self.settingsWindowObserver = nil
214	                        }
215	                    }
216	                }
217	            }
218	        }
219	    }
220	
221	    /// SwiftUI tags its Settings window with an identifier containing "Settings"
222	    /// (or "Preferences" on older SDKs). Match on the stable identifier rather
223	    /// than localized titles.
224	    private static func findSettingsWindow() -> NSWindow? {
225	        NSApp.windows.first { window in
226	            let identifier = window.identifier?.rawValue ?? ""
227	            return identifier.contains("Settings") || identifier.contains("Preferences")
228	        }
229	    }
230	
231	    /// Walk the main menu looking for the App menu's Settings… (or Preferences…)
232	    /// item and invoke it. Going through the menu guarantees the action is
233	    /// routed to whichever responder SwiftUI wired up, instead of relying on
234	    /// `sendAction(_:to:from:)` to resolve a nil target in the responder chain
235	    /// — which is unreliable right after an activation-policy switch.
236	    private static func triggerSettingsMenuItem() {
237	        guard let mainMenu = NSApp.mainMenu else { return }
238	        for topItem in mainMenu.items {
239	            guard let submenu = topItem.submenu else { continue }
240	            for (index, item) in submenu.items.enumerated() {
241	                let title = item.title
242	                if title.hasPrefix("Settings") || title.hasPrefix("Preferences") {
243	                    submenu.performActionForItem(at: index)
244	                    return
245	                }
246	            }
247	        }
248	    }
249	
250	    // MARK: - Quit
251	
252	    private func quit() {
253	        let pollerRef = poller
254	        let monitorRef = accountMonitor
255	        Task {
256	            await pollerRef?.stop()
257	            await monitorRef?.stop()
258	        }
259	        usageStore?.stop()
260	        pidGuard?.release()
261	        NSApplication.shared.terminate(nil)
262	    }
263	}

1	import Foundation
2	
3	// MARK: - Root
4	
5	public struct UsagesFile: Codable, Equatable, Sendable {
6	    public var usages: [VendorUsageEntry]
7	
8	    public init(usages: [VendorUsageEntry] = []) {
9	        self.usages = usages
10	    }
11	}
12	
13	// MARK: - Entry
14	
15	public struct VendorUsageEntry: Codable, Equatable, Sendable, Identifiable {
16	    public var id: String { "\(vendor.rawValue):\(account.rawValue)" }
17	
18	    public let vendor: Vendor
19	    public let account: AccountEmail
20	    public var isActive: Bool
21	    public var lastAcquiredOn: ISODate?
22	    public var lastError: UsageError?
23	    public var metrics: [UsageMetric]
24	
25	    public init(
26	        vendor: Vendor,
27	        account: AccountEmail,
28	        isActive: Bool = false,
29	        lastAcquiredOn: ISODate? = nil,
30	        lastError: UsageError? = nil,
31	        metrics: [UsageMetric] = []
32	    ) {
33	        self.vendor = vendor
34	        self.account = account
35	        self.isActive = isActive
36	        self.lastAcquiredOn = lastAcquiredOn
37	        self.lastError = lastError
38	        self.metrics = metrics
39	    }
40	}
41	
42	// MARK: - Error
43	
44	public struct UsageError: Codable, Equatable, Sendable {
45	    public let timestamp: ISODate
46	    public let type: String
47	
48	    public init(timestamp: ISODate, type: String) {
49	        self.timestamp = timestamp
50	        self.type = type
51	    }
52	}
53	
54	// MARK: - Metric (polymorphic on "type" discriminator)
55	
56	public enum UsageMetric: Codable, Equatable, Sendable {
57	    case timeWindow(name: String, resetAt: ISODate?, windowDuration: DurationMinutes, usagePercent: UsagePercent)
58	    case payAsYouGo(name: String, currentAmount: Double, currency: String)
59	    /// A metric type not yet known to this client; retained for round-trip fidelity.
60	    case unknown(String)
61	
62	    // MARK: Kind
63	
64	    public var kind: MetricKind {
65	        switch self {
66	        case .timeWindow:       .timeWindow
67	        case .payAsYouGo:       .payAsYouGo
68	        case .unknown(let t):   .unknown(t)
69	        }
70	    }
71	
72	    // MARK: Coding
73	
74	    private enum CodingKeys: String, CodingKey {
75	        case type, name, resetAt
76	        case windowDuration = "windowDurationMinutes"
77	        case usagePercent, currentAmount, currency
78	    }
79	
80	    public init(from decoder: Decoder) throws {
81	        let container = try decoder.container(keyedBy: CodingKeys.self)
82	        let metricKind = try container.decode(MetricKind.self, forKey: .type)
83	        switch metricKind {
84	        case .timeWindow:
85	            self = .timeWindow(
86	                name: try container.decode(String.self, forKey: .name),
87	                resetAt: try container.decodeIfPresent(ISODate.self, forKey: .resetAt),
88	                windowDuration: try container.decode(DurationMinutes.self, forKey: .windowDuration),
89	                usagePercent: try container.decode(UsagePercent.self, forKey: .usagePercent)
90	            )
91	        case .payAsYouGo:
92	            self = .payAsYouGo(
93	                name: try container.decode(String.self, forKey: .name),
94	                currentAmount: try container.decode(Double.self, forKey: .currentAmount),
95	                currency: try container.decode(String.self, forKey: .currency)
96	            )
97	        case .unknown(let t):
98	            self = .unknown(t)
99	        }
100	    }
101	
102	    public func encode(to encoder: Encoder) throws {
103	        var container = encoder.container(keyedBy: CodingKeys.self)
104	        switch self {
105	        case let .timeWindow(name, resetAt, windowDuration, usagePercent):
106	            try container.encode(MetricKind.timeWindow, forKey: .type)
107	            try container.encode(name, forKey: .name)
108	            try container.encodeIfPresent(resetAt, forKey: .resetAt)
109	            try container.encode(windowDuration, forKey: .windowDuration)
110	            try container.encode(usagePercent, forKey: .usagePercent)
111	        case let .payAsYouGo(name, currentAmount, currency):
112	            try container.encode(MetricKind.payAsYouGo, forKey: .type)
113	            try container.encode(name, forKey: .name)
114	            try container.encode(currentAmount, forKey: .currentAmount)
115	            try container.encode(currency, forKey: .currency)
116	        case let .unknown(t):
117	            // Only the type discriminator round-trips; unknown fields are not preserved
118	            try container.encode(MetricKind.unknown(t), forKey: .type)
119	        }
120	    }
121	}

1	import Foundation
2	import Observation
3	
4	// MARK: - Clock abstraction (testable time source)
5	
6	/// Abstracts `Date.now` so tests can inject a fixed point in time.
7	public protocol ClockProvider: Sendable {
8	    func now() -> Date
9	}
10	
11	public struct SystemClock: ClockProvider {
12	    public init() {}
13	    public func now() -> Date { Date() }
14	}
15	
16	// MARK: - Store
17	
18	/// One segment of the menu bar label — a single metric's formatted text and its tier.
19	public struct MenuBarSegment: Sendable {
20	    public let text: String
21	    public let tier: ConsumptionTier?
22	}
23	
24	/// Formats the active Claude account's time-window metrics for the menu bar.
25	/// Example: `S 48% 2h 13m | W 7% 6d 6h 13m`. Falls back to `"--"` when data is
26	/// unavailable, malformed, or no active account is found.
27	@Observable
28	@MainActor
29	public final class UsageStore {
30	    public private(set) var menuBarText: String = "--"
31	    public private(set) var menuBarTier: ConsumptionTier?
32	    public private(set) var menuBarSegments: [MenuBarSegment] = []
33	    /// Incremented each time handleNewData is called, whether the data is valid or malformed.
34	    /// Tests use this as a reliable signal that the store has consumed the latest yielded item.
35	    public private(set) var dataProcessedCount: Int = 0
36	    public private(set) var entries: [VendorUsageEntry] = []
37	
38	    // MARK: Dependencies
39	
40	    private let fileWatcher: FileWatching
41	    private let clock: ClockProvider
42	    private let logger: FileLogger
43	    private let countdownRefreshSeconds: UInt64
44	
45	    public static let defaultCountdownRefreshSeconds: UInt64 = 60
46	    private static let fallbackText = "--"
47	    private static let rawPreviewMaxBytes = 200
48	
49	    private static func rawPreview(_ data: Data) -> String {
50	        let slice = data.prefix(rawPreviewMaxBytes)
51	        if let text = String(data: slice, encoding: .utf8) {
52	            return "utf8:\(text.debugDescription)"
53	        }
54	        let hex = slice.map { String(format: "%02x", $0) }.joined()
55	        return "hex:\(hex)"
56	    }
57	
58	    private static let targetVendor: Vendor = .claude
59	    // Only top-level aggregate metrics belong in the compact menu bar label; per-model breakdowns live in the popover only
60	    private static let menuBarMetricNames: Set<String> = ["5h sessions (all models)", "Weekly (all models)"]
61	    // Explicit short abbreviations for menu bar display — descriptive names start with digits or lowercase
62	    private static let menuBarAbbreviations: [String: String] = [
63	        "5h sessions (all models)": "S",
64	        "Weekly (all models)": "W",
65	    ]
66	
67	    // Latest decoded file kept for countdown refresh without re-reading disk
68	    private var lastFile: UsagesFile?
69	
70	    private var watchTask: Task<Void, Never>?
71	    private var countdownTask: Task<Void, Never>?
72	
73	    public init(
74	        fileWatcher: FileWatching,
75	        clock: ClockProvider = SystemClock(),
76	        logger: FileLogger = Loggers.app,
77	        countdownRefreshSeconds: UInt64 = UsageStore.defaultCountdownRefreshSeconds
78	    ) {
79	        self.fileWatcher = fileWatcher
80	        self.clock = clock
81	        self.logger = logger
82	        self.countdownRefreshSeconds = countdownRefreshSeconds
83	    }
84	
85	    // MARK: Lifecycle
86	
87	    /// Start watching the file and refreshing the countdown.
88	    /// Idempotent — calling again is a no-op.
89	    public func start() {
90	        guard watchTask == nil else { return }
91	
92	        let watcher = fileWatcher
93	        // Self is captured strongly; stop() cancels both tasks to break the cycle
94	        watchTask = Task { [self] in
95	            for await data in watcher.changes() {
96	                guard !Task.isCancelled else { break }
97	                self.handleNewData(data)
98	            }
99	        }
100	
101	        countdownTask = Task { [self] in
102	            let refreshNanos = self.countdownRefreshSeconds * 1_000_000_000
103	            while !Task.isCancelled {
104	                // CancellationError swallowed intentionally; checked on next loop iteration
105	                try? await Task.sleep(nanoseconds: refreshNanos)
106	                guard !Task.isCancelled else { break }
107	                self.refreshMenuBarText()
108	            }
109	        }
110	    }
111	
112	    /// Stop all background tasks. Idempotent.
113	    public func stop() {
114	        watchTask?.cancel()
115	        watchTask = nil
116	        countdownTask?.cancel()
117	        countdownTask = nil
118	    }
119	
120	    // Defensive: cancels tasks when the store is released without an explicit stop() call.
121	    @MainActor deinit { stop() }
122	
123	    // MARK: - Processing
124	
125	    private func handleNewData(_ data: Data) {
126	        dataProcessedCount += 1
127	        do {
128	            let file = try JSONDecoder().decode(UsagesFile.self, from: data)
129	            lastFile = file
130	            entries = file.usages
131	            let result = format(file: file)
132	            menuBarText = result.text
133	            menuBarTier = result.tier
134	            menuBarSegments = result.segments
135	        } catch {
136	            let preview = Self.rawPreview(data)
137	            logger.log(.error, "UsageStore: JSON decode failed: \(error) — bytes=\(data.count), preview=\(preview)")
138	            lastFile = nil
139	            entries = []
140	            menuBarText = Self.fallbackText
141	            menuBarTier = nil
142	            menuBarSegments = []
143	        }
144	    }
145	
146	    private func refreshMenuBarText() {
147	        guard let file = lastFile else { return }
148	        let result = format(file: file)
149	        menuBarText = result.text
150	        menuBarTier = result.tier
151	        menuBarSegments = result.segments
152	    }
153	
154	    // MARK: - Formatting
155	
156	    private struct FormatResult {
157	        let segments: [MenuBarSegment]
158	        let text: String
159	        let tier: ConsumptionTier?
160	    }
161	
162	    private func format(file: UsagesFile) -> FormatResult {
163	        guard let entry = file.usages.first(where: {
164	            $0.vendor == Self.targetVendor && $0.isActive
165	        }) else {
166	            return FormatResult(segments: [], text: Self.fallbackText, tier: nil)
167	        }
168	
169	        let now = clock.now()
170	        var segments: [MenuBarSegment] = []
171	
172	        for metric in entry.metrics {
173	            guard let segment = formatTimeWindowSegment(metric, now: now) else { continue }
174	            segments.append(segment)
175	        }
176	
177	        let worstTier = segments.compactMap(\.tier).max()
178	        let text = segments.isEmpty ? Self.fallbackText : segments.map(\.text).joined(separator: " | ")
179	        return FormatResult(segments: segments, text: text, tier: segments.isEmpty ? nil : worstTier)
180	    }
181	
182	    private func formatTimeWindowSegment(_ metric: UsageMetric, now: Date) -> MenuBarSegment? {
183	        if case let .unknown(t) = metric {
184	            logger.log(.debug, "UsageStore: skipping unknown metric type '\(t)'")
185	        }
186	        guard case let .timeWindow(name, resetAt, windowDuration, usagePercent) = metric else {
187	            return nil
188	        }
189	        guard Self.menuBarMetricNames.contains(name) else { return nil }
190	
191	        let abbreviation = Self.menuBarAbbreviations[name] ?? String(name.prefix(1)).uppercased()
192	        // A metric with an empty name cannot be abbreviated — skip to avoid a leading space in output
193	        guard !abbreviation.isEmpty else { return nil }
194	
195	        let remaining = resetAt.map { formatRemainingTime(resetAt: $0, now: now) } ?? "???"
196	        let text = "\(abbreviation) \(usagePercent.rawValue)% \(remaining)"
197	
198	        let theoretical = resetAt.map { theoreticalFraction(resetAt: $0, windowDuration: windowDuration, now: now) } ?? 0.0
199	        let tier = consumptionRatio(actualPercent: usagePercent, theoreticalFraction: theoretical)
200	            .map { consumptionTier(ratio: $0) }
201	
202	        return MenuBarSegment(text: text, tier: tier)
203	    }
204	
205	    private func formatRemainingTime(resetAt: ISODate, now: Date) -> String {
206	        guard resetAt.date != nil else {
207	            logger.log(.warning, "UsageStore: invalid ISO8601 resetAt value: \(resetAt.rawValue)")
208	            return "--"
209	        }
210	        return AIUsagesTrackersLib.formatRemainingTime(resetAt: resetAt, now: now)
211	    }
212	}

This skill guides creation of distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces that avoid generic "AI slop" aesthetics. Implement real working code with exceptional attention to aesthetic details and creative choices.

## Context Gathering Protocol

Design skills produce generic output without project context. You MUST have confirmed design context before doing any design work.

**Required context** (every design skill needs at minimum):
- **Target audience**: Who uses this product and in what context?
- **Use cases**: What jobs are they trying to get done?
- **Brand personality/tone**: How should the interface feel?

Individual skills may require additional context. Check the skill's preparation section for specifics.

**CRITICAL**: You cannot infer this context by reading the codebase. Code tells you what was built, not who it's for or what it should feel like. Only the creator can provide this context.

**Gathering order:**
1. **Check current instructions (instant)**: If your loaded instructions already contain a **Design Context** section, proceed immediately.
2. **Check .impeccable.md (fast)**: If not in instructions, read `.impeccable.md` from the project root. If it exists and contains the required context, proceed.
3. **Run impeccable teach (REQUIRED)**: If neither source has context, you MUST run /impeccable teach NOW before doing anything else. Do NOT skip this step. Do NOT attempt to infer context from the codebase instead.

---

## Design Direction

Commit to a BOLD aesthetic direction:
- **Purpose**: What problem does this interface solve? Who uses it?
- **Tone**: Pick an extreme: brutally minimal, maximalist chaos, retro-futuristic, organic/natural, luxury/refined, playful/toy-like, editorial/magazine, brutalist/raw, art deco/geometric, soft/pastel, industrial/utilitarian, etc. There are so many flavors to choose from. Use these for inspiration but design one that is true to the aesthetic direction.
- **Constraints**: Technical requirements (framework, performance, accessibility).
- **Differentiation**: What makes this UNFORGETTABLE? What's the one thing someone will remember?

**CRITICAL**: Choose a clear conceptual direction and execute it with precision. Bold maximalism and refined minimalism both work. The key is intentionality, not intensity.

Then implement working code that is:
- Production-grade and functional
- Visually striking and memorable
- Cohesive with a clear aesthetic point-of-view
- Meticulously refined in every detail

## Frontend Aesthetics Guidelines

### Typography
→ *Consult [typography reference](reference/typography.md) for OpenType features, web font loading, and the deeper material on scales.*

Choose fonts that are beautiful, unique, and interesting. Pair a distinctive display font with a refined body font.

<typography_principles>
Always apply these — do not consult a reference, just do them:

- Use a modular type scale with fluid sizing (clamp) for headings on marketing/content pages. Use fixed `rem` scales for app UIs and dashboards (no major design system uses fluid type in product UI).
- Use fewer sizes with more contrast. A 5-step scale with at least a 1.25 ratio between steps creates clearer hierarchy than 8 sizes that are 1.1× apart.
- Line-height scales inversely with line length. Narrow columns want tighter leading, wide columns want more. For light text on dark backgrounds, ADD 0.05-0.1 to your normal line-height — light type reads as lighter weight and needs more breathing room.
- Cap line length at ~65-75ch. Body text wider than that is fatiguing.
</typography_principles>

<font_selection_procedure>
DO THIS BEFORE TYPING ANY FONT NAME.

The model's natural failure mode is "I was told not to use Inter, so I will pick my next favorite font, which becomes the new monoculture." Avoid this by performing the following procedure on every project, in order:

Step 1. Read the brief once. Write down 3 concrete words for the brand voice (e.g., "warm and mechanical and opinionated", "calm and clinical and careful", "fast and dense and unimpressed", "handmade and a little weird"). NOT "modern" or "elegant" — those are dead categories.

Step 2. List the 3 fonts you would normally reach for given those words. Write them down. They are most likely from this list:

<reflex_fonts_to_reject>
Fraunces
Newsreader
Lora
Crimson
Crimson Pro
Crimson Text
Playfair Display
Cormorant
Cormorant Garamond
Syne
IBM Plex Mono
IBM Plex Sans
IBM Plex Serif
Space Mono
Space Grotesk
Inter
DM Sans
DM Serif Display
DM Serif Text
Outfit
Plus Jakarta Sans
Instrument Sans
Instrument Serif
</reflex_fonts_to_reject>

Reject every font that appears in the reflex_fonts_to_reject list. They are your training-data defaults and they create monoculture across projects.

Step 3. Browse a font catalog with the 3 brand words in mind. Sources: Google Fonts, Pangram Pangram, Future Fonts, Adobe Fonts, ABC Dinamo, Klim Type Foundry, Velvetyne. Look for something that fits the brand as a *physical object* — a museum exhibit caption, a hand-painted shop sign, a 1970s mainframe terminal manual, a fabric label on the inside of a coat, a children's book printed on cheap newsprint. Reject the first thing that "looks designy" — that's the trained reflex too. Keep looking.

Step 4. Cross-check the result. The right font for an "elegant" brief is NOT necessarily a serif. The right font for a "technical" brief is NOT necessarily a sans-serif. The right font for a "warm" brief is NOT Fraunces. If your final pick lines up with your reflex pattern, go back to Step 3.
</font_selection_procedure>

<typography_rules>
DO use a modular type scale with fluid sizing (clamp) on headings.
DO vary font weights and sizes to create clear visual hierarchy.
DO vary your font choices across projects. If you used a serif display font on the last project, look for a sans, monospace, or display face on this one.

DO NOT use overused fonts like Inter, Roboto, Arial, Open Sans, or system defaults — but also do not simply switch to your second-favorite. Every font in the reflex_fonts_to_reject list above is banned. Look further.
DO NOT use monospace typography as lazy shorthand for "technical/developer" vibes.
DO NOT put large icons with rounded corners above every heading. They rarely add value and make sites look templated.
DO NOT use only one font family for the entire page. Pair a distinctive display font with a refined body font.
DO NOT use a flat type hierarchy where sizes are too close together. Aim for at least a 1.25 ratio between steps.
DO NOT set long body passages in uppercase. Reserve all-caps for short labels and headings.
</typography_rules>

### Color & Theme
→ *Consult [color reference](reference/color-and-contrast.md) for the deeper material on contrast, accessibility, and palette construction.*

Commit to a cohesive palette. Dominant colors with sharp accents outperform timid, evenly-distributed palettes.

<color_principles>
Always apply these — do not consult a reference, just do them:

- Use OKLCH, not HSL. OKLCH is perceptually uniform: equal steps in lightness *look* equal, which HSL does not deliver. As you move toward white or black, REDUCE chroma — high chroma at extreme lightness looks garish. A light blue at 85% lightness wants ~0.08 chroma, not the 0.15 of your base color.
- Tint your neutrals toward your brand hue. Even a chroma of 0.005-0.01 is perceptible and creates subconscious cohesion between brand color and UI surfaces. The hue you tint toward should come from THIS brand, not from a "warm = friendly" or "cool = tech" formula. Pick the brand's actual hue first, then tint everything toward it.
- The 60-30-10 rule is about visual *weight*, not pixel count. 60% neutral / surface, 30% secondary text and borders, 10% accent. Accents work BECAUSE they're rare. Overuse kills their power.
</color_principles>

<theme_selection>
Theme (light vs dark) should be DERIVED from audience and viewing context, not picked from a default. Read the brief and ask: when is this product used, by whom, in what physical setting?

- A perp DEX consumed during fast trading sessions → dark
- A hospital portal consumed by anxious patients on phones late at night → light
- A children's reading app → light
- A vintage motorcycle forum where users sit in their garage at 9pm → dark
- An observability dashboard for SREs in a dark office → dark
- A wedding planning checklist for couples on a Sunday morning → light
- A music player app for headphone listening at night → dark
- A food magazine homepage browsed during a coffee break → light

Do not default everything to light "to play it safe." Do not default everything to dark "to look cool." Both defaults are the lazy reflex. The correct theme is the one the actual user wants in their actual context.
</theme_selection>

<color_rules>
DO use modern CSS color functions (oklch, color-mix, light-dark) for perceptually uniform, maintainable palettes.
DO tint your neutrals toward your brand hue. Even a subtle hint creates subconscious cohesion.

DO NOT use gray text on colored backgrounds; it looks washed out. Use a shade of the background color instead.
DO NOT use pure black (#000) or pure white (#fff). Always tint; pure black/white never appears in nature.
DO NOT use the AI color palette: cyan-on-dark, purple-to-blue gradients, neon accents on dark backgrounds.
DO NOT use gradient text for impact — see <absolute_bans> below for the strict definition. Solid colors only for text.
DO NOT default to dark mode with glowing accents. It looks "cool" without requiring actual design decisions.
DO NOT default to light mode "to be safe" either. The point is to choose, not to retreat to a safe option.
</color_rules>

### Layout & Space
→ *Consult [spatial reference](reference/spatial-design.md) for the deeper material on grids, container queries, and optical adjustments.*

Create visual rhythm through varied spacing, not the same padding everywhere. Embrace asymmetry and unexpected compositions. Break the grid intentionally for emphasis.

<spatial_principles>
Always apply these — do not consult a reference, just do them:

- Use a 4pt spacing scale with semantic token names (`--space-sm`, `--space-md`), not pixel-named (`--spacing-8`). Scale: 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64, 96. 8pt is too coarse — you'll often want 12px between two values.
- Use `gap` instead of margins for sibling spacing. It eliminates margin collapse and the cleanup hacks that come with it.
- Vary spacing for hierarchy. A heading with extra space above it reads as more important — make use of that. Don't apply the same padding everywhere.
- Self-adjusting grid pattern: `grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(280px, 1fr))` is the breakpoint-free responsive grid for card-style content.
- Container queries are for components, viewport queries are for page layout. A card in a sidebar should adapt to the sidebar's width, not the viewport's.
</spatial_principles>

<spatial_rules>
DO create visual rhythm through varied spacing: tight groupings, generous separations.
DO use fluid spacing with clamp() that breathes on larger screens.
DO use asymmetry and unexpected compositions; break the grid intentionally for emphasis.

DO NOT wrap everything in cards. Not everything needs a container.
DO NOT nest cards inside cards. Visual noise; flatten the hierarchy.
DO NOT use identical card grids (same-sized cards with icon + heading + text, repeated endlessly).
DO NOT use the hero metric layout template (big number, small label, supporting stats, gradient accent).
DO NOT center everything. Left-aligned text with asymmetric layouts feels more designed.
DO NOT use the same spacing everywhere. Without rhythm, layouts feel monotonous.
DO NOT let body text wrap beyond ~80 characters per line. Add a max-width like 65–75ch so the eye can track easily.
</spatial_rules>

### Visual Details

<absolute_bans>
These CSS patterns are NEVER acceptable. They are the most recognizable AI design tells. Match-and-refuse: if you find yourself about to write any of these, stop and rewrite the element with a different structure entirely.

BAN 1: Side-stripe borders on cards/list items/callouts/alerts
  - PATTERN: `border-left:` or `border-right:` with width greater than 1px
  - INCLUDES: hard-coded colors AND CSS variables
  - FORBIDDEN: `border-left: 3px solid red`, `border-left: 4px solid #ff0000`, `border-left: 4px solid var(--color-warning)`, `border-left: 5px solid oklch(...)`, etc.
  - WHY: this is the single most overused "design touch" in admin, dashboard, and medical UIs. It never looks intentional regardless of color, radius, opacity, or whether the variable name is "primary" or "warning" or "accent."
  - REWRITE: use a different element structure entirely. Do not just swap to box-shadow inset. Reach for full borders, background tints, leading numbers/icons, or no visual indicator at all.

BAN 2: Gradient text
  - PATTERN: `background-clip: text` (or `-webkit-background-clip: text`) combined with a gradient background
  - FORBIDDEN: any combination that makes text fill come from a `linear-gradient`, `radial-gradient`, or `conic-gradient`
  - WHY: gradient text is decorative rather than meaningful and is one of the top three AI design tells
  - REWRITE: use a single solid color for text. If you want emphasis, use weight or size, not gradient fill.
</absolute_bans>

DO: Use intentional, purposeful decorative elements that reinforce brand.
DO NOT: Use border-left or border-right greater than 1px as a colored accent stripe on cards, list items, callouts, or alerts. See <absolute_bans> above for the strict CSS pattern.
DO NOT: Use glassmorphism everywhere (blur effects, glass cards, glow borders used decoratively rather than purposefully).
DO NOT: Use sparklines as decoration. Tiny charts that look sophisticated but convey nothing meaningful.
DO NOT: Use rounded rectangles with generic drop shadows. Safe, forgettable, could be any AI output.
DO NOT: Use modals unless there's truly no better alternative. Modals are lazy.

### Motion
→ *Consult [motion reference](reference/motion-design.md) for timing, easing, and reduced motion.*

Focus on high-impact moments: one well-orchestrated page load with staggered reveals creates more delight than scattered micro-interactions.

**DO**: Use motion to convey state changes: entrances, exits, feedback
**DO**: Use exponential easing (ease-out-quart/quint/expo) for natural deceleration
**DO**: For height animations, use grid-template-rows transitions instead of animating height directly
**DON'T**: Animate layout properties (width, height, padding, margin). Use transform and opacity only
**DON'T**: Use bounce or elastic easing. They feel dated and tacky; real objects decelerate smoothly

### Interaction
→ *Consult [interaction reference](reference/interaction-design.md) for forms, focus, and loading patterns.*

Make interactions feel fast. Use optimistic UI: update immediately, sync later.

**DO**: Use progressive disclosure. Start simple, reveal sophistication through interaction (basic options first, advanced behind expandable sections; hover states that reveal secondary actions)
**DO**: Design empty states that teach the interface, not just say "nothing here"
**DO**: Make every interactive surface feel intentional and responsive
**DON'T**: Repeat the same information (redundant headers, intros that restate the heading)
**DON'T**: Make every button primary. Use ghost buttons, text links, secondary styles; hierarchy matters

### Responsive
→ *Consult [responsive reference](reference/responsive-design.md) for mobile-first, fluid design, and container queries.*

**DO**: Use container queries (@container) for component-level responsiveness
**DO**: Adapt the interface for different contexts, not just shrink it
**DON'T**: Hide critical functionality on mobile. Adapt the interface, don't amputate it

### UX Writing
→ *Consult [ux-writing reference](reference/ux-writing.md) for labels, errors, and empty states.*

**DO**: Make every word earn its place
**DON'T**: Repeat information users can already see

---

## The AI Slop Test

**Critical quality check**: If you showed this interface to someone and said "AI made this," would they believe you immediately? If yes, that's the problem.

A distinctive interface should make someone ask "how was this made?" not "which AI made this?"

Review the DON'T guidelines above. They are the fingerprints of AI-generated work from 2024-2025.

---

## Implementation Principles

Match implementation complexity to the aesthetic vision. Maximalist designs need elaborate code with extensive animations and effects. Minimalist or refined designs need restraint, precision, and careful attention to spacing, typography, and subtle details.

Interpret creatively and make unexpected choices that feel genuinely designed for the context. No design should be the same. Vary between light and dark themes, different fonts, different aesthetics. NEVER converge on common choices across generations.

Remember: Claude is capable of extraordinary creative work. Don't hold back. Show what can truly be created when thinking outside the box and committing fully to a distinctive vision.

---

## Craft Mode

If this skill is invoked with the argument "craft" (e.g., `/impeccable craft [feature description]`), follow the [craft flow](reference/craft.md). Pass any additional arguments as the feature description.

---

## Teach Mode

If this skill is invoked with the argument "teach" (e.g., `/impeccable teach`), skip all design work above and instead run the teach flow below. This is a one-time setup that gathers design context for the project.

### Step 1: Explore the Codebase

Before asking questions, thoroughly scan the project to discover what you can:

- **README and docs**: Project purpose, target audience, any stated goals
- **Package.json / config files**: Tech stack, dependencies, existing design libraries
- **Existing components**: Current design patterns, spacing, typography in use
- **Brand assets**: Logos, favicons, color values already defined
- **Design tokens / CSS variables**: Existing color palettes, font stacks, spacing scales
- **Any style guides or brand documentation**

Note what you've learned and what remains unclear.

### Step 2: Ask UX-Focused Questions

STOP and call the AskUserQuestion tool to clarify. Focus only on what you couldn't infer from the codebase:

#### Users & Purpose
- Who uses this? What's their context when using it?
- What job are they trying to get done?
- What emotions should the interface evoke? (confidence, delight, calm, urgency, etc.)

#### Brand & Personality
- How would you describe the brand personality in 3 words?
- Any reference sites or apps that capture the right feel? What specifically about them?
- What should this explicitly NOT look like? Any anti-references?

#### Aesthetic Preferences
- Any strong preferences for visual direction? (minimal, bold, elegant, playful, technical, organic, etc.)
- Light mode, dark mode, or both?
- Any colors that must be used or avoided?

#### Accessibility & Inclusion
- Specific accessibility requirements? (WCAG level, known user needs)
- Considerations for reduced motion, color blindness, or other accommodations?

Skip questions where the answer is already clear from the codebase exploration.

### Step 3: Write Design Context

Synthesize your findings and the user's answers into a `## Design Context` section:

```markdown
## Design Context

### Users
[Who they are, their context, the job to be done]

### Brand Personality
[Voice, tone, 3-word personality, emotional goals]

### Aesthetic Direction
[Visual tone, references, anti-references, theme]

### Design Principles
[3-5 principles derived from the conversation that should guide all design decisions]
```

Write this section to `.impeccable.md` in the project root. If the file already exists, update the Design Context section in place.

Then STOP and call the AskUserQuestion tool to clarify. whether they'd also like the Design Context appended to CLAUDE.md. If yes, append or update the section there as well.

Confirm completion and summarize the key design principles that will now guide all future work.

If this skill is invoked with the argument "extract" (e.g., `/impeccable extract [target]`), follow the [extract flow](reference/extract.md). Pass any additional arguments as the extraction target.

Boutons monter/descendre + supprimer (avec confirm). Ordre des champs figé. Bouton "Add segment" en draft. Si account disparaît: warning + proposer suppression. content.text:
Pour ce contexte (Settings window native macOS), on suit la HIG Apple plutôt qu'une direction esthétique bold — `Form { Section }`, contrôles système, pas de typographie custom. Le travail UX se concentre sur la structure/interaction, pas l'aesthetic.

## Maquette ASCII — tab "Menubar hint"

```
┌─ Menubar hint ─────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                    │
│  Preview                                           │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  ● S 2% 1h47m  │  ● W 48% 3d 11h            │    │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                    │
│  Segments                              [+ Add]     │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ ⋮⋮  ▶  ● S 2% 1h47m              ↑ ↓  ⌫    │    │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┤    │
│  │ ⋮⋮  ▼  ● W 48% 3d 11h            ↑ ↓  ⌫    │    │
│  │     ┌────────────────────────────────────┐ │    │
│  │     │ Vendor    [ Claude          ▼ ]   │ │    │
│  │     │ Account   [ frederic@4sh.fr ▼ ]   │ │    │
│  │     │ Metric    [ Weekly (all…)   ▼ ]   │ │    │
│  │     │                                    │ │    │
│  │     │ Display                            │ │    │
│  │     │   ◉ Colored dot                    │ │    │
│  │     │   ◉ Metric letter (W)              │ │    │
│  │     │   ◉ Usage percentage               │ │    │
│  │     │   ○ Time until reset               │ │    │
│  │     └────────────────────────────────────┘ │    │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┤    │
│  │ ⋮⋮  ▶  ⚠ Account no longer available  ⌫   │    │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Choix UX clés

**1. `DisclosureGroup` plutôt que `List` + sheet d'édition**
   - L'édition inline évite un mode modal et garde le preview visible
   - Le titre collapsed = preview live du segment → feedback immédiat des toggles

**2. Ordre : drag-and-drop ET boutons ↑/↓**
   - SwiftUI `.onMove` dans une `List` gère le drag natif (poignée `⋮⋮` à gauche)
   - Garder ↑/↓ explicites pour accessibilité clavier/VoiceOver
   - Seul l'ordre des **segments** est réordonnable, pas les champs internes

**3. Segments draft (nouveau)**
   - `[+ Add]` crée immédiatement un segment expandé avec vendor=premier dispo, account=premier, metric=première
   - Pas de "draft state" séparé : un segment est valide dès création (toujours des valeurs par défaut sensées)
   - Si `usages.json` est vide → bouton `[+ Add]` désactivé avec tooltip "Wait for first poll"

**4. Preview en haut du tab**
   - Rendu identique à la menubar (même font, mêmes couleurs)
   - Update live à chaque modif → l'utilisateur valide visuellement avant de fermer

**5. Edge case "account disparu"**
   - Titre collapsed : `⚠ Account no longer available` (rouge système)
   - Expand : message explicatif + bouton "Remove segment" en évidence
   - Le segment est skip dans le rendu menubar (pas d'erreur silencieuse)

**6. Confirmation suppression**
   - `.confirmationDialog` natif (sheet sliding macOS) plutôt qu'`alert` modal
   - Texte : "Delete this segment?" + "This will remove '● W 48% 3d 11h' from the menu bar."

**7. Dropdowns dépendants**
   - Account dropdown filtré par vendor sélectionné
   - Metric dropdown filtré par vendor+account
   - Si l'utilisateur change vendor → reset account+metric sur premier dispo (pas de combo invalide)

**8. Toggle "lettre" — quelle lettre ?**
   - Auto-dérivée de la metric via la map existante (`menuBarAbbreviations`)
   - Affichée entre parenthèses dans le label : `Metric letter (W)` → l'utilisateur sait ce qu'il active

**9. État vide (zéro segment)**
   - Texte centré : "No segments configured. The menu bar will show '--'."
   - Bouton `[+ Add your first segment]` proéminent

## Points qui appellent une décision de ta part

- **Preview top** : utile ou redondant avec la menubar elle-même (déjà visible) ? Je penche pour le garder — quand on édite, l'œil reste dans la fenêtre.
- **`menuBarAbbreviations`** est actuellement hardcodée pour 2 noms. On la déplace en computed property du metric, ou on garde la map et on tombe en fallback sur `name.prefix(1)` ? (l'UI doit afficher quelque chose de stable)
- **Persistance** : segments stockés en JSON dans `UserDefaults` (clé unique `menuBarSegments`) ou un fichier dédié ? Je recommande `UserDefaults` (cohérent avec le reste).

Confirme ces 3 derniers points + valide la maquette, et je passe à l'implémentation (modèles + UI + branchement avec `UsageStore.format`).

content.text:
Bien noté. Synthèse finale du modèle :

**Segment**
```
- vendor: Vendor
- account: AccountSelection { .currentlyActive | .specific(AccountEmail) }
- metricName: String
- display: SegmentDisplay  (variant dépendant du metric kind, déterminé au render)
    - .timeWindow(showDot: Bool, letter: String?, showPercent: Bool, showReset: Bool)
    - .payAsYouGo  (no config — affiche montant+currency)
```

**Account dropdown — toujours en tête : "Currently active account"**, puis liste des accounts avec ≥1 métrique pour le vendor sélectionné.

**Metric dropdown** : metrics du couple résolu à l'édition (active account résolu au moment de l'ouverture du picker). Filtré aux metrics disponibles.

**Rendu expandé selon metric kind** :
- `timeWindow` → 4 toggles + champ texte "Letter" (default = `menuBarAbbreviations[name] ?? name.prefix(1).uppercased()`, max 3 chars)
- `payAsYouGo` → juste un texte d'info "Will display: $12.50" (rien à configurer)

**Migration au 1er lancement** : 2 segments avec `account = .currentlyActive`, vendor=claude, metrics S et W, tous toggles ON, lettres S et W.

**Edge cases** :
- `.currentlyActive` mais aucun account actif → warning "No active account"
- `.specific(email)` mais email absent du JSON → warning "Account no longer available" + bouton remove
- Metric absente sur l'account résolu → warning "Metric '...' not found on this account"

---

# SwiftLint configuration for AIUsagesTrackers.
#
# Rule codes mirror .claude/rules/swift-quality.md — keep the mapping in sync.
# Severities: `error` fails the build; `warning` surfaces in logs / IDE.

included:
  - Sources
  - Tests

# Narrow the SwiftLint surface to exactly the codes documented in
# .claude/rules/swift-quality.md. Built-in rules are deliberately NOT enabled —
# we want a low-noise, high-signal lint focused on the patterns that reviews
# keep flagging. Reach for built-in rules only if a review finding recurs that
# a custom rule cannot express precisely.
only_rules:
  - custom_rules

custom_rules:
  e1_try_silencing_critical_fs:
    name: "try? on critical filesystem op"
    regex: 'try\?\s+(FileManager\.default\.)?(createDirectory|moveItem|copyItem|setAttributes)\b'
    message: "try? swallows failure on a correctness-critical FS op — use do/catch (SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING §1)"
    severity: error

  e2_flock_lock_ex_without_nb:
    name: "flock LOCK_EX without LOCK_NB"
    regex: 'flock\([^)]*LOCK_EX(?![^)]*LOCK_NB)'
    message: "flock with LOCK_EX must be OR'd with LOCK_NB and wrapped in a timeout loop (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY / SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS)"
    severity: error
    excluded:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w1_nonisolated_unsafe:
    name: "nonisolated(unsafe) in production"
    regex: 'nonisolated\(unsafe\)'
    excluded_match_kinds:
      - comment
      - doccomment
    message: "nonisolated(unsafe) bypasses Swift isolation — prefer actor-isolated storage or per-call allocation (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY)"
    severity: warning
    excluded:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w3_task_sleep_literal_in_tests:
    name: "Task.sleep with fixed literal in tests"
    regex: 'Task\.sleep\([^)]*[0-9]'
    message: "Task.sleep with a fixed literal is fragile under CI load — poll the observable state with the eventually() helper (SWIFT-TESTABILITY)"
    severity: warning
    included:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w4_unchecked_sendable:
    name: "@unchecked Sendable"
    regex: '@unchecked\s+Sendable'
    excluded_match_kinds:
      - comment
      - doccomment
    message: "@unchecked Sendable requires an adjacent justification comment describing the single-threaded contract (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY)"
    severity: warning

  w5_duration_literal_in_comment:
    name: "Duration literal embedded in comment"
    regex: '\b\d+\s*(hours?|days?|minutes?|seconds?|ms)\b'
    match_kinds:
      - comment
    message: "Comment embeds a bare `N unit` duration — lift to a named constant whose declaration site carries the rationale (SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Comment WHY)"
    severity: warning

5	// MARK: - Test doubles
6	
7	/// A mock FileWatching that lets tests push arbitrary Data payloads.
8	/// Implemented as a class so `changes()` can hand out a fresh AsyncStream on each
9	/// call — required for start/stop/start lifecycle tests where the previous iteration
10	/// is cancelled before a new watchTask begins consuming again.
11	// swiftlint:disable:next w4_unchecked_sendable — all test access is single-threaded on the main actor; AsyncStream.Continuation is Sendable
12	final class MockFileWatcher: FileWatching, @unchecked Sendable {
13	    private var continuation: AsyncStream<Data>.Continuation?
14	    /// Buffers sends that arrive before the watch task calls changes(), or after a
15	    /// cancelled previous cycle. Flushed immediately when changes() is called.
16	    private var pendingData: [Data] = []
17	
18	    func changes() -> AsyncStream<Data> {
19	        // Finish the previous stream (if any) before creating a new one.
20	        // This prevents a stale task from consuming the new continuation's yields.
21	        continuation?.finish()
22	        var cont: AsyncStream<Data>.Continuation!
23	        let stream = AsyncStream<Data> { cont = $0 }
24	        continuation = cont
25	        // Flush sends that arrived before this call (e.g. test called send() before
26	        // the watch task had a chance to call changes()).
27	        for data in pendingData { cont.yield(data) }
28	        pendingData.removeAll()
29	        return stream
30	    }
31	
32	    func send(_ data: Data) {
33	        guard let cont = continuation else {
34	            pendingData.append(data)
35	            return
36	        }
37	        // .terminated means the previous cycle's consuming task was cancelled;
38	        // buffer the data so the next changes() call can deliver it.
39	        if case .terminated = cont.yield(data) {
40	            continuation = nil
41	            pendingData.append(data)
42	        }
43	    }
44	
45	    func finish() { continuation?.finish() }
46	}

content.text:
# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.
J'analyse l'état du repo et les changements en cours.

9bfab6c fix(settings): open Settings window on first cog click from .accessory app
.accessory apps have no dock presence; AppKit doesn't wire the
showSettingsWindow: action to the responder chain until the menu bar is
fully built. On first invocation, sendAction() silently fails because:

1. The standard App menu (where Settings… is registered) hasn't been
   installed yet during the .accessory → .regular transition
2. nil target routing through the responder chain finds no responder

Solution: find and programmatically trigger the Settings… menu item via
performActionForItem(at:), which routes the action through the menu's
already-wired target. On subsequent clicks, the Settings window already
exists in memory, so we skip the menu lookup and reuse it directly.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: ce4812be847b

63e8b34 feat(ui): make popover responsive to screen height
Replace fixed popover size (400px) with dynamic sizing based on screen
dimensions. Popover now grows up to 90% of visible screen height; scrollbar
appears only when content exceeds this cap.

Changes:
- NSHostingController uses .preferredContentSize sizing mode
- ScrollView maxHeight computed from 90% of screen height minus chrome

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(settings): redesign Settings window with configurable menubar segments + Assistants tab

Refactor Settings from 2 generic tabs (General, Logging) into 3 focused tabs:

General tab:
  - Moved "Startup > Launch at login" to the top
  - Moved "Logging > Log level" to the bottom (removed dedicated tab)

Assistants tab (new):
  - Section "Claude" with single slider control for API polling interval
  - 5 fixed steps: 1/2/3/5/10 minutes (default: 3 min)
  - Replaces the continuous 30s–30min slider, improving UX clarity

Menubar hint tab (new):
  - Live preview of rendered menubar label (respects dark/light appearance)
  - Configurable segments via vendor/account/metric dropdowns (cascade-filtered)
  - Per-segment display options:
    • timeWindow: colored dot, metric letter, usage %, time until reset (4 toggles)
    • payAsYouGo: displays "amount currency" (no toggles)
  - Account selection: "Currently active account" + specific email options
  - Filters out vendor/account pairs with no metrics; account dropdown respects this
  - Segments reorderable via drag-and-drop + ↑↓ buttons (keyboard accessible)
  - Delete with confirmation dialog
  - Inline warnings for broken segments (noActiveAccount, accountNotFound, etc.)
  - Empty state with call-to-action

Core architecture:
  - MenuBarSegmentConfig value object (Codable) + AccountSelection enum
  - SegmentDisplay variant: timeWindow/payAsYouGo with display options
  - MenuBarSegmentResolver: config + entries → rendered segment or issue
  - Persistance: JSON in UserDefaults (key: menuBarSegments)
  - Auto-seed on first launch: S + W segments on active Claude account
  - UsageStore refactored to iterate config instead of hardcoded metric names
  - Observation tracking for settings changes (manual access/withMutation on computed props)

Tests:
  - MenuBarSegmentConfig: 14 tests (Codable round-trip, variants)
  - MenuBarSegmentResolver: 14 tests (account resolution, display toggles, edge cases)
  - MenuBarSegmentsSeeder: 5 tests (fresh install, re-seed guard, persistence)
  - AppPreferences: 6 new tests (menuBarSegments persistence, corruption handling)
  - All 270 existing tests pass

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
